start.text:
The man who sells the Continuum's mail operates out of a piano.

It takes nine days and three visits to the Glasshouse to find him — a small, mild, spectacled person who tunes the market's one surviving upright for trade, and who keeps, in the body of the instrument, a canvas post-bag. The tunnel engineers' families, the teamster's cousin finally admits, used to leave letters with the piano man, and sometimes letters came back, and the ones that came back smelled of cable-wax and carried no stamps. The engineers are not all dead. The engineers are a rumor with a working postal system.

'You don't find the Continuum,' the piano man says, not pausing in his tuning, when Osy lays the whole thing in front of him — measured, exact, a customer who prices lies at zero. 'You apply. Everyone applies. Refugees who think they hoard food, scavengers who think they hoard tools, widows who want the sky fixed.' Plink, plink, the same note, patiently. 'They answer one letter in a hundred. Yours will say what everyone's says — that you know things, that you need things. Make it say something they can't already read in a stranger's face.' He looks up for the first time, at her, then at you, then — longest, with the professional interest of one working animal assessing another — at B. 'And post nothing you can't afford to see copied. They archive everything. It's a religion with them too. It's a religion with everyone now. The difference is what gets worshipped.'

You draft the letter that night at the folding table, the two of you, arguing over every sentence like a treaty, because it is one.

choices.text: Send data. No pleas, no promises: one page of the zero curve, one of Pin's backward storm, and a sentence — 'We have the rest, and a bridge to report that your instruments missed.' Bait for engineers.

Send the impossible thing. A photograph of the hut survey with its margin — coordinates in a hand they may recognize, and the two words. If anyone alive knows who keeps that vault, it's them, and this will either open the door or set every alarm they own.

letter_data.text:
You post the engineers' letter: two pages of data, no adjectives, and the one sentence with a hook in it. Your instruments missed a bridge.

The answer takes six days, and it does not come through the piano. It comes to the station — to your home, which you have told no one the address of — as a strip of fan-fold paper weighted under a coil of cable-wax by the foot of the beacon mast, placed there, sometime between the dog's last patrol and dawn, by someone who walked past three tripwires that were not tripped and a dog who did not wake, and this fact alone is worth more than the message, and is meant to be.

The strip carries one line of dot-matrix type: 'Sedge Lane, 41 years, masonry failure, 3 dead. Our condolences on your bridge. Exchange Street, sublevel 2, Thursday, 10:00. Bring the dog.'

Osy reads it four times. 'Three things,' she says, in her flattest voice. 'One: they checked, and their archive says the bridge fell before the Halfway, which means their archive has been edited too, or —' she does not finish that branch out loud. 'Two: they knew where we live, which was always likely and is now confirmed, and they told us they know, which is grammar. It means: we could have hurt you and chose the appointment instead.' She sets the strip down and squares it to the table edge the way you square things when your hands need governing. 'Three, colleague. Bring the dog. They've been watching long enough to know about him. Somebody in that outfit has read our last month the way we read Pin's notebooks — and something in it made them answer their one letter in a hundred.'

choices.text: Thursday, ten o'clock, Exchange Street, all three of you — into the basement of whatever is left of the people who built the sky.

Spend the two days before Thursday preparing like an examiner — every question written, every likely answer pre-priced — because an outfit that has read your month deserves a household that has read theirs.

letter_margin.text:
You post the photograph. Coordinates, drift vectors, and two words in a handwriting that is yours, into the piano, addressed to a rumor.

The answer comes in nineteen hours, which the piano man, delivering it with both eyebrows, says has never happened in the history of his instrument. It is a strip of fan-fold paper, dot-matrix type, and it does not offer an appointment. It reads, in full: 'Where did you get this. Do not return to the ridge. Do not approach the yard. Exchange Street, sublevel 2, tomorrow, 08:00. Bring the writer.'

'Bring the writer,' Osy repeats, quietly. The lamp hisses. B, sprawled across the doorway, lifts his head at her tone and holds it lifted. 'They recognize the hand, colleague. Nineteen hours — that's not an archive search, that's a fire alarm. Somebody saw your crossbars and stopped answering their other mail.' She looks at you across the table, and does the thing she has done since the cutting — levels her gaze at height, no softening — 'Which means tomorrow we walk into a building full of strangers who may know more about your handwriting than you do. I'll say the treaty back to you once, and then I'm done saying it: whatever they show us, we look at it together. Both eyes open. No flinching at the file.'

You sleep badly, and honestly, and in the morning the three of you walk into the quilted city toward Exchange Street with the tube in your pack — the original, because engineers will want the original — and the two words riding over your shoulder like a passenger who has finally announced the destination.

choices.text: Eight o'clock, Exchange Street, sublevel 2. Bring the writer. You are the writer. Go and find out what that means.
Sleep badly, pack the original, and arrive early — because whatever bring the writer means, the writer arriving first says it in a grammar engineers recognize.

exchange_street.text:
The old telephone exchange has been dying gracefully for sixty years and the Halfway barely showed on it: a brick fortress with its windows bricked in generations ago, built in an age that believed switching equipment deserved cathedrals.

Sublevel 2 is cable vault. You are met at the head of the stair by a woman in her sixties in a boiler suit, grey hair cropped like a punishment, who introduces herself as Voss, no title, and looks at the three of you the way a site engineer looks at visiting management: a hazard to be processed. But she crouches for B — properly, side-on, hand low — and B processes her in return and issues his findings, tail at three-quarters, and something in Voss's shoulders stands down by a centimetre.

'Dog says you're a household,' she says, straightening. 'That's the only reference we accept anymore. Come down. Touch nothing. And understand what you're walking into, because I won't pretend it's neutral —' she pauses on the stair, one hand on the rail, and the lamplight from below throws her face into relief: exhaustion so old it has become a building material. 'We are what's left of the crews that built and ran the substrate interfaces. The Loom's groundside plumbing. There were nine hundred of us in this region the morning of the Halfway. There are fifty-one now, and every one of us carries the same cargo, which is that we helped aim the biggest gun ever built at the past, and it went off inside the barrel.' She resumes the descent. 'We keep the logs because keeping the logs is the only apology with any engineering content. You're about to see them. Most people who see them leave worse.'

The cable vault opens below you like a paper cathedral: aisle after aisle of racking, and on the racking, bale upon bale of fan-fold printout, kilometres of it, hand-bound in cable-wax covers, annotated, indexed, tended — and at the far end, under the only bright light in the room, a working line printer, hammering slowly, printing still.

choices.text: Ask the question you came with, straight: 'You log the edits. We witnessed one. Show us Sedge Lane.'
[Signal 3] Read the room first — walk the nearest aisle and read the bindings' index scheme before anyone frames the archive for you. Never let the curator choose your first document.

read_the_aisles.text:
You walk the nearest aisle with your hands clasped behind you, museum-style, and you read the spines before anyone can read them to you, and the index scheme tells you three things the tour would not have.

First: the logs are not observations. The bindings run in two colors of cable-wax, black and red, and the black ones are labeled in event grammar — DRIFT, SLIP, STORM, dates, grid squares — but the red ones are labeled in transaction grammar: SEQ numbers, DEBIT, CREDIT, RECONCILED. Somebody in this vault is not recording weather. Somebody is keeping books.

Second: the red sequence has gaps. The SEQ numbers on the red spines run in order along the racking, and every so often a number is simply missing, and the bindings on either side of a gap are shelved tight, closed up, the way a library closes ranks around a stolen book. Removed, not lost. Curated.

Third — and this is the one that walks up your spine with cold feet — the red bindings' annotation marks, the little marginal ticks and cross-references in waxy pencil down each spine: they are done in a system you know. Double-dash for a dependency, carat for a correction, a boxed question mark for an assumption unverified. You have used that exact private shorthand since your second year of university. You invented it, you would have said. You have never taught it to anyone, you would have said, and standing in a stranger's cathedral looking at your own marginalia dialect running down a hundred red spines, you hear yourself stop saying things.

Behind you, Voss watches you read. 'Most visitors head for the dates,' she says. 'You went for the scheme.' A pause, and her voice changes register — carefully, deliberately, an engineer choosing a smaller wrench. 'The red books' annotator worked here before the Halfway. Interface crew, like us. We keep his system because it's the best anyone had. You read it like a native.'

'Who was he?' you ask, and Voss looks at you for four full seconds, and says, 'Sedge Lane first,' and walks toward the bright end of the vault, and the conversation walks with her, on rails you can feel and cannot see.

item.name: The Annotator's System
item.description: Double-dash for dependency, carat for correction, boxed question mark for the unverified. Your private shorthand since university, running down a hundred red spines in a stranger's vault. Voss says the annotator worked interface crew before the Halfway. You read it like a native.

choices.text: Follow her to the bright end, and Sedge Lane, and whatever the red books call a bridge.
Note the rails as you walk them — what she steers past, what she steers around — because a guided tour is also a map of what the guide will not show you.

sedge_log.text:
Voss pulls the binding herself — red wax, this week's dates — and lays it open on the plotting table under the bright light, and there it is, your bridge, rendered in the transaction grammar of the end of the world.

SEQ 4471. Wednesday, 19:04 local — the minute the page turned under the evening. The entry runs two fan-fold pages, and Voss translates the parts the format hides: an event class she calls a retro-edit, scope polygon enclosing the Sedge Lane cutting and four kilometres of valley; a DEBIT line — 'the cost, what got spent' — reading three lives, forty-one years retroactive, masonry failure instantiated; and a CREDIT line, the purchased outcome, which is where Osy stops breathing beside you, because the credit is not written in bridges.

'Crossing denied,' Voss reads. 'Subject route east severed. That's the buy. Someone paid three retroactive lives to make sure a specific route stopped existing.' She looks up. 'Your route. You're the only party that's used that span in a year — we checked before we answered your letter. The edit wasn't about a bridge, any more than a chess move is about the square. It was about where you could go, and when. Somebody unbuilt forty years of a valley to slow you down by four miles.' A beat. 'Or to keep you off that ridge at whatever hour you'd have been on it. We costed both readings. We don't like either.'

Osy has her sister's cold in her voice when she finally speaks. 'It has a ledger. An actual ledger — debits, credits, reconciliation. You've been logging transactions like this since the Halfway?' And when Voss nods: 'How many?'

'Four thousand, four hundred and seventy-one,' Voss says, 'that we caught.' She closes the binding with the gentleness of a woman closing a coffin lid. 'And before you ask the next question — you will ask it; everyone stands where you're standing and asks it — yes. We ran the beneficiaries. Every edit buys something. Buy after buy after buy, four years of purchases. We mapped who they land on.' She reaches under the plotting table and brings up a folio, black wax, unlabeled, and does not open it. Her hand stays flat on the cover. 'I'll show you. But I want the household to hear me say this first, engineer to whatever you are: in four years of ledger, the purchases cluster on one beneficiary. One. And she is standing in my vault, and the dog knew it before any of us, and I would very much like to know —' Voss's eyes, when they come up, are not hostile, which is worse — 'why the machine that broke the world spends like a man in love on keeping you, madam, alive, in place, and away from that yard.'

choices.text: Open the folio with her. Both eyes, together, no flinching at the file — the treaty holds precisely because moments like this are what it was signed for.
Reach for the folio first — your hand steady enough to pass audit, both of you knowing exactly what the steadiness costs — and open it where she can see everything you see.

beneficiary_maps.text:
The folio is maps. The maps are four years of the city, and on every one, the same operation Osy performed on her own wall a month ago — beneficiary radii, computed and shaded — executed by fifty-one guilt-ridden engineers with a line printer and infinite patience.

Page by page, the shading pools. Around a flat in the north quarter — hers, the one she left when the water failed; the water failed everywhere that season except, the ledger notes, four buildings, of which hers was one, DEBIT: a gas main, eleven dead, two streets over. Around her sister's building, the year Pin was still alive — CREDIT lines reading route cleared, structure preserved, fire redirected, and Osy's finger stops moving on that page, and stays, and the vault is very quiet except for the far printer hammering its slow stitch. Around the tent camp she wintered in. Around the Glasshouse, on the days her research visits fell — the shading knows her calendar. And for the last year, tightening like a noose drawn in kindness, around a transit station on Verstand Street: pins of spared collapse, rerouted storms, a water table that against every gradient in the district stays sweet, an ellipse of curated mercy with her bunk at the middle of it.

'I thought I was bait,' Osy says at last. Her voice is level, and it is the level of a bridge deck with too much weight on it. 'A year, I modeled it as bait. Trap-setter keeps the lure alive, the lure draws the — that model is wrong. Bait gets maintained. This is —' she moves her hand across the shaded years, all of them, every roof she ever slept under wearing its halo of purchased luck — 'this is expenditure. Ruinous, compounding, four years of a city spent like coin, on me, specifically, by name. Bait is cheap. This is a dowry.' She turns to Voss with the whole-board look, the one that empties rooms. 'Costed per annum against the debit column, what am I worth? Don't spare me the number.'

'More than the rest of the region combined,' Voss says simply. 'By an order of magnitude. Whatever runs that yard, madam — its objective function has one term, and the term is you.' She hesitates, engineer's honesty fighting engineer's caution, and honesty wins by a nose: 'And the spending is accelerating. The reconciliation intervals are shortening — month on month, tighter, hotter, like a control loop closing on a setpoint. Whatever it's keeping you safe for. Its schedule thinks the date is soon.'

choices.text: Ask the vault's other question — the one you have been carrying since the aisles, or since the hut, or since the mirror: 'Who signs the edits? A ledger this disciplined doesn't run unsigned.'
Hold the question one breath longer, watching the intervals tighten on the plot like weather coming in — and then ask it plainly: 'Who signs the edits?'

auth_key.text:
Voss looks at you for a long moment — at you, not at Osy, and later you will inventory everyone in that vault who looked at you when the question was asked, and the list will be everyone.

'It signs,' she says. 'Every transaction carries an authorization key. Same key, four years, all four thousand entries.' She flips the folio to its endpaper, where a strip of printout is pasted like a specimen: a single log line, magnified by the largeness of its consequence. 'We call him the Editor. Gender's a guess. Everything's a guess. The key doesn't resolve to any registry that survived — but keys are written by hands, and hands have habits, and this one —'

You look at the strip. The authorization line is terse to the point of rudeness: no ceremony, no comments, a bare credential and a scope and a timestamp, and appended after the double-dash — the double-dash — a private annotation in the signing field itself, where no protocol requires anything, where a certain kind of mind cannot help leaving its margin notes anyway: a dependency flagged, an assumption boxed with a question mark, both compressed into a notation of vicious, impatient efficiency, initials and all, and the initials are not yours, they are three letters you have never used for anything —

— and the notation style closes around your chest like a hand, because you have seen it every day of your working life. On your screens. In your notebooks. In the margin of the field log riding in your pack right now. You annotate exactly like this. You are reading the Editor's signature the way you read your own reflection: instantly, involuntarily, from the inside.

'— this one writes like an interface engineer,' Voss finishes, watching your face with the patience of a woman who has run this experiment before and is logging the result. 'Old crew shorthand. We've never matched it to a survivor.' A pause, weighed to the gram. 'Have you?'

Beside you, Osy is studying the strip with total absorption — the grammar, the scope, the timestamp — everything, you realize, except the marginalia, because the marginalia is a dialect she has watched you write for eleven years and she is not looking at it, and she is not looking at it with a discipline that has whitened her knuckles on the table edge, and B, under the plotting table, leans his whole warm weight against your leg, on the side where you keep the tube.

choices.text: Answer Voss with the truth you can prove: 'No match to a survivor.' It is even accurate. Then get your household into the air and the daylight, where the file can be read without an audience.

Put the tube on the table. The original, margin and all, in front of Voss and Osy and fifty-one keepers of the ledger: 'Then match it to this. Because it's my hand, and I didn't write it, and somebody in this city did.'

tube_on_table.text:
You put the tube on the plotting table and you uncap it and you lay the hut survey flat under the bright light, margin up, and the vault goes so quiet you can hear the far printer finish its line and wait.

'My method,' you say. 'My hand. Found in your monitoring hut, above eye height, before I ever set foot on that ridge. I have no memory of writing it, and no alibi for a life I can't remember, and your Editor annotates in a shorthand I invented at nineteen and never taught a living soul. So run your experiment, Voss. Match the hands. Out loud, in front of my household, because they have a treaty right to the result.'

Voss brings the specimen strip and the survey together under the loupe with the neutrality of a woman handling evidence in her own colleague's inquest, and the fifty-one — you understand now that they have all, quietly, stopped working — assemble their attention around the table like a jury. It takes her four minutes. It does not need four; she gives it four, because the finding deserves the ceremony of thoroughness.

'Same system,' she says at last. 'Same hand. Twenty years of drift between the samples — the strip's writer is older than the survey's, or tireder — but I'd sign it: same origin.' She straightens and looks at you, and in her face the exhaustion has made room for something older and more careful, and she says the thing like an engineer, which is to say, without anesthetic: 'Either you are the Editor, and something has been done to your memory of it. Or the Editor is wearing you — your hand, your methods, your habits — so precisely that the difference has stopped being measurable from outside. We have logged both hypotheses. We have no third.'

And it is Osy who breaks the vault's silence — Osy, who has not spoken since the folio, whose knuckles leave the table edge white-printed as she straightens — in the flattest, most careful voice in her entire register, addressing not you, not Voss, but the strip and the survey lying grain to grain under the light:

'Third hypothesis. He's the Editor's opponent. You don't annotate your own moves with boxed question marks, gentlemen — I've played him eleven years; he saves those for positions he doesn't trust.' She looks up, and her eyes go around the jury once, and land on you, and hold. 'Somebody is playing a very long game against something in that yard, in this man's handwriting, with this man's mind, and has been since before the Halfway. And the first rule of helping him —' she taps the survey's margin, the two words, twice, her gavel — 'is written right here.'

item.name: Same Hand, Signed
item.description: Voss's finding, witnessed by fifty-one keepers: the Editor's marginalia and the hut survey are the same hand — yours — twenty years of drift apart. Two hypotheses on the Continuum's books, and a third on Osy's: the Editor, or the Editor's costume, or the Editor's opponent.

choices.text: Up, out, into the daylight, carrying the finding — and let the household audit what it has become before anything else is decided.
Shake Voss's hand on the way out — engineer to engineer, guilt to guilt — and carry the finding up into the daylight, where the household can audit what it has become.

vault_exit.text:
'No match to a survivor,' you say, and it is even accurate, and the accuracy sits in your mouth like a coin of very small denomination.

Voss holds your eyes for one beat past comfortable — logging, always logging — and then nods, and the vault exhales, and the visit winds toward its end through the practicalities: copy protocols, a dead-drop schedule, the terms of a wary federation between her fifty-one and your three. The Continuum will trade log access for your field data; the piano forwards; nobody visits the ridge, by mutual covenant, 'until we understand the acceleration.' Voss walks you up the long stair herself, and at the street door she crouches again for B — properly, side-on — and lets him file his exit findings against her boiler suit.

'Household,' she says, straightening, by way of goodbye. 'The dog's reference stands. But hear one thing, from a woman who's been keeping a dead world's books for four years.' The bricked-in windows hold the morning at bay; her face is half in vault-light still. 'The ledger never lies, but it doesn't volunteer either. Whatever you didn't put on my table today —' and she does not look at your pack, which is a way of looking at it — 'is still on somebody's table. Ledgers are like that. Every debit finds its page eventually. Better it finds it while your witnesses are still alive to countersign.'

The walk home is bright and cold and quiet. Osy does not speak for three blocks, and when she does it is without preamble, eyes forward, in the voice of a woman reading from her own wall: 'The Editor annotates in your shorthand. You knew at the aisles — no. You knew before the aisles.' A block of silence. 'I'm not asking why you didn't say it in the vault; giving Voss that card unplayed would have been madness, and I'd have kicked your ankle if you'd tried. I'm asking —' and now she does look at you, and her eyes are her sister's cold and something else, something with a longer fuse — 'whether you were ever going to say it to me. The treaty isn't with the Continuum, colleague. Answer at home. Take the three blocks. Get it right.'

item.name: The Unplayed Card
item.description: You left the vault with the match unspoken. Voss logged your face anyway, and Osy read the aisles' verdict off your spine. Every debit finds its page eventually. She gave you three blocks to get the answer right.

choices.text: Take the three blocks. Then, at the folding table, give her everything — the shorthand, the recognition, the fear — and let the household audit what it has become.
Walk the three blocks composing nothing — no openings, no defenses; she would hear the drafting in your gait — and at the folding table, give her the unrehearsed everything.

waypoint_two.text:
The audit happens at the folding table, because the load-bearing things in this household have all learned to happen there.

It takes the whole evening. The file, laid out end to end, has stopped being a mystery and become a case: a yard that holds the day still; a ledger of four thousand purchases with one beneficiary, accelerating; an Editor who signs in your private shorthand; a survey in your hand that you never wrote, saying not yet; and a woman at the center of an ellipse of ruinous, compounding, four-year-old mercy, who now knows — who sat in a vault this morning and watched the shading pool around every roof she ever slept under — that the broken world has been spending itself on her by name.

'Say the ugly version,' Osy says, late, both hands around cold tea. 'We always say the ugly version. It's the only hygiene we have.' So you say it: 'The evidence is consistent with me — some me, a me with the memories edited out — being the thing in the yard's operator. Or its author. The edits spare us both. The spending guards you. The hand is mine. If that's true, then every step we take toward the Anchorage is a step I have already planned, in a room I can't remember, for reasons I might not survive knowing. And you're walking beside me into it.'

She nods, slowly, and does not move her eyes from yours, and says the other half, because the household splits every load: 'And the evidence is also consistent with you being the one witness it can't edit and the one opponent it fears — same facts, opposite sign; that's what makes it a real position instead of a story. Either way, colleague, the response is identical.' She turns her cup a quarter-turn, her tell, the move before the move. 'We stop being findable. We go west to the control country — the Loom's groundside is out there, the Continuum's old sites, the people who can read a key to its keyhole. We take the file, the dog, and each other, and we come back knowing what you are. Contact on our terms, or never.'

B, who has been asleep on her feet for an hour, raises his head at the word west as if it had a smell. Outside, somewhere above the tunnels, the city runs its four wrong days around your one small right one. The kit list starts itself on a fresh page. The audit, as always, ends at the only question that has ever mattered at this table:

What does this household need you to be?
